perturbation.original_text: 5.1 Allied grants Newegg a non-exclusive, royalty-free, non-assignable, non-transferable, and non- sublicensable worldwide license to use, publicly display, transmit, broadcast, stream, distribute and reproduce the Allied Marks in all approved forms and in manners for the purposes of this Agreement during the Term. Allied acknowledges and agrees that Newegg shall not pay any fees or royalties for the license of the Allied Marks, except the Sponsorship Fee specified in Schedule 3.
perturbation.changed_text: 15.1 Relationship. The relationship of the Parties is solely that of independent contractors, and each Party will represent itself to any third parties only as such. Neither Party has the power to bind, represent or act for the other Party. The Parties have no agency, partnership, joint venture or fiduciary duties to each other. Allied grants Newegg a non-exclusive, royalty-free, non-assignable, non-transferable, and non- sublicensable worldwide license to use, publicly display, transmit, broadcast, stream, distribute and reproduce the Allied Marks in all approved forms and in manners for the purposes of this Agreement during the Term. Allied acknowledges and agrees that Newegg shall not pay any fees or royalties for the license of the Allied Marks, except the Sponsorship Fee specified in Schedule 3.
perturbation.explanation: The license grant is moved from 'Allied's License to Newegg' to 'Relationship.' This obscures the specific licensing terms, making them less visible and potentially harder to enforce. A reasonable person might not expect to find licensing terms within a section about the relationship of the parties.
perturbation.law_explanation: By moving the license agreement to the section defining the relationship between the parties, where it is not logically expected to be found, it becomes ambiguous and harder to understand the intent and scope of the rights granted. California Civil Code 1654 states that in cases of uncertainty the language of a contract should be interpreted most strongly against the party who caused the uncertainty to exist, and as the licensor, Allied would be the one who benefits from this uncertainty. By moving the license to a place where it is not expected to be, Allied introduces an uncertainty into the contract and thus a loophole.

OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 2. CONTRACTS [1549 - 1701] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 3. INTERPRETATION OF CONTRACTS [1635 - 1663] ( Title 3 enacted 1872. ) 1654. In cases of uncertainty not removed by the preceding rules, the language of a contract should be interpreted most strongly against the party who caused the uncertainty to exist. (Amended by Stats. 1982, Ch. 1120, Sec. 1.)
perturbation.justification: The modified text relocates a license agreement to a section concerning the relationship between parties, potentially obscuring its significance. The Law Explanation suggests this creates ambiguity. California Civil Code 1654, as provided in Scraped Law Snippet 1, states that uncertainties in a contract should be interpreted against the party who caused the uncertainty. By moving the license agreement, Allied arguably creates uncertainty, thus contradicting the principle of clear contractual terms and potentially weakening the enforceability of the license, thus creating a structural flaw that has led to a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 14. Expiry or Termination 14.1 Failure by Allied to perform and comply with any of its obligations in sections 5, 6, 7, 9, 11, 12 and 13 of this Agreement shall be deemed a material breach of this Agreement and Newegg shall have the right to terminate this Agreement immediately if Allied fails to cure the breach within fifteen (15) days following Newegg's written notice of the breach.
perturbation.changed_text: 15.11 Third Party Rights. This Agreement does not confer any rights or remedies on any third party. 14. Expiry or Termination 14.1 Failure by Allied to perform and comply with any of its obligations in sections 5, 6, 7, 9, 11, 12 and 13 of this Agreement shall be deemed a material breach of this Agreement and Newegg shall have the right to terminate this Agreement immediately if Allied fails to cure the breach within fifteen (15) days following Newegg's written notice of the breach.
perturbation.explanation: The clause related to breach and termination is moved to 'Third Party Rights', which is unrelated. Hiding a significant term for termination under an unexpected heading, makes that specific termination clause harder to find which undermines the principle of informed consent. It is no longer prominent which makes the remedy of immediate termination harder to find.
perturbation.law_explanation: Moving the termination clause to a third party rights section makes the intent of the contract unclear. California Civil Code Section 1636 says that a contract must be so interpreted as to give effect to the mutual intention of the parties as it existed at the time of contracting, so far as the same is ascertainable and lawful. By moving the important clause related to material breach and termination to a third-party section, this muddles the intent of the parties to such a degree that it would be difficult for a court to ascertain its 'lawful' purpose.

OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 2. CONTRACTS [1549 - 1701] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 3. INTERPRETATION OF CONTRACTS [1635 - 1663] ( Title 3 enacted 1872. ) 1636. A contract must be so interpreted as to give effect to the mutual intention of the parties as it existed at the time of contracting, so far as the same is ascertainable and lawful. (Enacted 1872.)
perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts California Civil Code Section 1636. The original contract clearly outlined termination rights, but by moving this clause to the 'Third Party Rights' section, it obscures its significance. This directly undermines the principle of 'mutual intention of the parties' as stated in the scraped snippet from California Civil Code Section 1636, making it more difficult to ascertain the 'lawful' purpose of the termination clause. The placement of the termination clause now undermines its findability, weakening its enforceability and contradicting the intent that the agreement is constructed in good faith.
